title: Senusret II

infobox.value: Co-regent of five years; sole reign of around fifteen years.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimal1992166-1)[[note 1]](#cite_note-10)
[Pyramid of Senusret II](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Senusret_II "Pyramid of Senusret II")

or **Sesostris II** (died c. 1878 BC) was the fourth [king](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") of the [12th Dynasty of Egypt](/wiki/Twelfth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Twelfth dynasty of Egypt"). [His pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Senusret_II "Pyramid of Senusret II") was constructed at [El-Lahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun"). Senusret II took a great deal of interest in the [Faiyum](/wiki/Faiyum "Faiyum") oasis region and began work on an extensive irrigation system from [Bahr Yussef](/wiki/Bahr_Yussef "Bahr Yussef") through to [Lake Moeris](/wiki/Lake_Moeris "Lake Moeris") through the construction of a dike at [El-Lahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun") and the addition of a network of drainage canals. The purpose of his project was to increase the amount of cultivable land in that area.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVerner2002386-12) The importance of this project is emphasized by Senusret II's decision to move the royal necropolis from [Dahshur](/wiki/Dahshur "Dahshur") to El-Lahun where he built his [pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Senusret_II "Pyramid of Senusret II"). This location would remain the political capital for the 12th and 13th Dynasties of Egypt. Senusret II was known by his prenomen **Khakheperre**, which means "The Ka of Re comes into being". The king also established the first known workers' quarter in the nearby town of Senusrethotep ([Kahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun")).[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetrie18915ff-13)

**Senusret II** or **Sesostris II** (died c. 1878 BC) was the fourth [king](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") of the [12th Dynasty of Egypt](/wiki/Twelfth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Twelfth dynasty of Egypt"). [His pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Senusret_II "Pyramid of Senusret II") was constructed at [El-Lahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun"). Senusret II took a great deal of interest in the [Faiyum](/wiki/Faiyum "Faiyum") oasis region and began work on an extensive irrigation system from [Bahr Yussef](/wiki/Bahr_Yussef "Bahr Yussef") through to [Lake Moeris](/wiki/Lake_Moeris "Lake Moeris") through the construction of a dike at [El-Lahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun") and the addition of a network of drainage canals. The purpose of his project was to increase the amount of cultivable land in that area.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVerner2002386-12) The importance of this project is emphasized by Senusret II's decision to move the royal necropolis from [Dahshur](/wiki/Dahshur "Dahshur") to El-Lahun where he built his [pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Senusret_II "Pyramid of Senusret II"). This location would remain the political capital for the 12th and 13th Dynasties of Egypt. Senusret II was known by his prenomen **Khakheperre**, which means "The Ka of Re comes into being". The king also established the first known workers' quarter in the nearby town of Senusrethotep ([Kahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun")).[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetrie18915ff-13)

## Reign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reign")]

### Co-regency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Co-regency")]

Co-regencies are a major issue for Egyptologists' understanding of the history of the Middle Kingdom and the 12th Dynasty.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECallender2004137–138-14)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimpson2001453-15) The Egyptologist [Claude Obsomer](/wiki/Claude_Obsomer "Claude Obsomer") wholly rejects the possibility of co-regencies in the 12th Dynasty.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2006170-16) The Egyptologist Karl Jansen-Winkeln[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen-Winkeln1997115–135-17) having investigated Obsomer's work concluded in favour of co-regencies.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2006170–171-18) Jansen-Winkeln cites a rock stele found at Konosso as irrefutable evidence in favour of a co-regency between Senusret II and Amenemhat II, and by extension proof of co-regencies in the 12th Dynasty.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen-Winkeln1997188–189-19) The Egyptologist [William Murnane](/wiki/William_J._Murnane "William J. Murnane") states that "the co-regencies of the period are all known ... from double-dated[[note 2]](#cite_note-21) documents".[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurnane19777-22) The Egyptologist Thomas Schneider concludes that recently discovered documents and archaeological evidence are effectively proof of co-regencies in this period.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2006171-23)

Some sources ascribe a co-regency period to Senusret II's rule, with Amenemhat II as his co-regent. The Egyptologist [Peter Clayton](/wiki/Peter_A._Clayton "Peter A. Clayton") ascribes at least three years of co-regency to Senusret II's reign.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199482-24) The Egyptologist Nicolas Grimal assigns nearly five years of co-regency prior to sole accession to the throne.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimal1992166-1)

### Length of reign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Length of reign")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/GuardianStatueofAmenemhmatII.jpg/250px-GuardianStatueofAmenemhmatII.jpg)](/wiki/File:GuardianStatueofAmenemhmatII.jpg)

A figure wearing the red crown of Lower Egypt and whose face appears to reflect the features of the reigning king, most probably [Amenemhat II](/wiki/Amenemhat_II "Amenemhat II") or Senusret II. It functioned as a divine guardian for the [imiut](/wiki/Imiut_fetish "Imiut fetish"), and it is wearing a divine kilt, which suggests that the statuette was not merely a representation of the living ruler.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

The lengths of the reigns of Senusret II and [Senusret III](/wiki/Senusret_III "Senusret III") are one of the main considerations for discerning the chronology of the 12th Dynasty.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimpson2001453-15) The [Turin Canon](/wiki/Turin_Canon "Turin Canon") is believed to assign a reign of 19 years to Senusret II and 30 years of reign to Senusret III.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyholt199714-26) This traditional view was challenged in 1972 when the Egyptologist [William Simpson](/wiki/William_Kelly_Simpson "William Kelly Simpson") observed that the latest attested regnal year for Senusret II was his 7th, and similarly for Senusret III his 19th.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyholt199714-26)

[Kim Ryholt](/wiki/Kim_Ryholt "Kim Ryholt"), a professor of Egyptology at the [University of Copenhagen](/wiki/University_of_Copenhagen "University of Copenhagen"), suggests the possibility that the names on the canon had been misarranged and offers two possible regnal lengths for Senusret II: 10+ years, or 19 years.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyholt199714–15-27) Several Egyptologists, such as [Thomas Schneider](/wiki/Thomas_Schneider_(Egyptologist) "Thomas Schneider (Egyptologist)"), cite Mark C. Stone's 1997 article in the *Göttinger Miszellen* as determining that Senusret II's highest recorded regnal year was his 8th, based on Stela Cairo JE 59485.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2006172_citing_[[#CITEREFStone1997|Stone_(1997]],_pp. 91–100)-28)

Some scholars prefer to ascribe him a reign of only 10 years and assign the 19-year reign to [Senusret III](/wiki/Senusret_III "Senusret III") instead. Other Egyptologists, however, such as [Jürgen von Beckerath](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_von_Beckerath "Jürgen von Beckerath") and [Frank Yurco](/wiki/Frank_Yurco "Frank Yurco"), have maintained the traditional view of a longer 19-year reign for Senusret II given the level of activity undertaken by the king during his reign.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Yurco notes that reducing Senusret II's regnal length to 6 years poses difficulties because:

> That pharaoh built a complete pyramid at [Kahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun"), with a solid granite funerary temple and complex of buildings. Such projects optimally took fifteen to twenty years to complete, even with the mudbrick cores used in Middle Kingdom pyramids.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYurco201469_citing_[[#CITEREFEdwards1985|Edwards_(1985]],_pp. 98_&_292)_and_[[#CITEREFGrimal1992|Grimal_(1992]],_pp. 166_&_391)-29)

At present, the problem concerning the reign length of Senusret II is irresolvable but many Egyptologists today prefer to assign him a reign of 9 or 10 years only given the absence of higher dates attested for him beyond his 8th regnal year. This would entail amending the 19-year figure which the Turin Canon assigns for a 12th dynasty ruler in his position to 9 years instead. However, Senusret II's monthly figure on the throne might be ascertained. According to [Jürgen von Beckerath](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_von_Beckerath "Jürgen von Beckerath"), the temple documents of [El-Lahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun"), the pyramid city of Sesostris/Senusret II often mention the Festival of "Going Forth to Heaven" which might be the date of death for this ruler.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Beckerath1995447-30) These documents state that this Festival occurred on IV Peret day 14.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBorchardt189991-31)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardiner194521–22-32)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimpsonn.d.LA_V900-33) However, Rita Gautschy states that this Festival date did not mark the actual day of Senusret II's death, but of his funeral or burial.[[32]](#cite_note-34) Lisa Saladino Haney observes based on Gautschy's interpretation of this date, "Therefore, if one takes seventy days from the feast day IV Peret 14, one gets II Peret 4 as the approximate date of death of Senwosret/[Senusret II].[[33]](#cite_note-35)

### Domestic activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Domestic activities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Uraeus_provenant_d%27une_couronne_royale_de_Sesostris_II_%28la_Villette%2C_2023%29.jpg/250px-Uraeus_provenant_d%27une_couronne_royale_de_Sesostris_II_%28la_Villette%2C_2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uraeus_provenant_d%27une_couronne_royale_de_Sesostris_II_(la_Villette,_2023).jpg)

Senusret II's royal [uraeus](/wiki/Uraeus "Uraeus") found in his pyramid tomb

The [Faiyum Oasis](/wiki/Faiyum_Oasis "Faiyum Oasis"), a region in Middle Egypt, has been inhabited by humans for more than 8,000 years.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilfong2001496-36) It became an important centre in Egypt during the Middle Kingdom.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilfong2001496-36) Throughout the period, rulers undertook developmental projects turning Faiyum into an agricultural, religious, and resort-like centre.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilfong2001496-36) The oasis was located 80 km (50 mi) south-west of Memphis offering arable land[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimal1992166-1) centred around Lake Moeris, a natural body of water.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilfong2001496-36)

Senusret II initiated a project to exploit the marshy region's natural resources for hunting and fishing, a project continued by his successors and which "matured" during the reign of his grandson [Amenemhat III](/wiki/Amenemhat_III "Amenemhat III").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimal1992166-1) To set off this project, Senusret II developed an irrigation system with a dyke and a network of canals which siphoned water from Lake Moeris.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECallender2004152-9)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimal1992166-1) The land reclaimed in this project was then farmed.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECallender2004152–153-37)

Cults honouring the crocodile god [Sobek](/wiki/Sobek "Sobek") were prominent at the time.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilfong2001496-36)

### Activities outside Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Activities outside Egypt")]

Senusret II's reign ushered in a period of peace and prosperity, with no recorded military campaigns and the proliferation of trade between Egypt and the Near-East.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECallender2004152-9)

Around the same time, parties of Western Asiatic foreigners visiting the King with gifts are recorded, as in the tomb paintings of [12th-dynasty](/wiki/12th_dynasty "12th dynasty") official [Khnumhotep II](/wiki/Khnumhotep_II "Khnumhotep II"), who also served under Senusret III. These foreigners, possibly [Canaanites](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") or [Bedouins](/wiki/Bedouins "Bedouins"), are labelled as *[Aamu](/wiki/Aamu "Aamu")* (*ꜥꜣmw*), including the leading man with a [Nubian ibex](/wiki/Nubian_ibex "Nubian ibex") labelled as *Abisha the Hyksos* (**𓋾𓈎𓈉** ḥḳꜣ-ḫꜣsw, *Heqa-kasut* for "Hyksos"), the first known instance of the name "[Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos")".[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_de_Mieroop2011131-38)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBard2015188-39)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamrin200925-40)[[39]](#cite_note-archaeology.org-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Procession_of_the_Aamu%2C_Tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_%28composite%29.jpg/500px-Procession_of_the_Aamu%2C_Tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_%28composite%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Procession_of_the_Aamu,_Tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_(composite).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Drawing_of_the_procession_of_the_Aamu_group_tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_at_Beni_Hassan.jpg/500px-Drawing_of_the_procession_of_the_Aamu_group_tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_at_Beni_Hassan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drawing_of_the_procession_of_the_Aamu_group_tomb_of_Khnumhotep_II_at_Beni_Hassan.jpg)

A group of West Asiatic foreigners bringing gift to the King. They are possibly [Canaanites](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan"), labelled as *[Aamu](/wiki/Aamu "Aamu")* (*ꜥꜣmw*), including the leading man with a [Nubian ibex](/wiki/Nubian_ibex "Nubian ibex") labelled as *Abisha the Hyksos* (**𓋾𓈎𓈉** ḥḳꜣ-ḫꜣsw, *Heqa-kasut* for "[Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos")"). Tomb of [12th-dynasty](/wiki/12th_dynasty "12th dynasty") official [Khnumhotep II](/wiki/Khnumhotep_II "Khnumhotep II"), who served under Senusret II, at [Beni Hasan](/wiki/Beni_Hasan "Beni Hasan") (c. 1900 BC).[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_de_Mieroop2011131-38)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBard2015188-39)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamrin200925-40)[[39]](#cite_note-archaeology.org-41)

### Royal Court

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Royal Court")]

#### Nomarchs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Nomarchs")]

Unlike his successor, Senusret II maintained good relations with the various [nomarchs](/wiki/Nomarch "Nomarch") or provincial governors of Egypt who were almost as wealthy as the king.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199483-42) His Year 6 is attested in a wall painting from the tomb of a local nomarch named [Khnumhotep II](/wiki/Khnumhotep_II "Khnumhotep II") at [Beni Hasan](/wiki/Beni_Hasan "Beni Hasan").

* [Khnumhotep II](/wiki/Khnumhotep_II "Khnumhotep II") (UE 16)

## Succession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Succession")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Pectoral_of_Senusret_II_by_John_Campana.jpg/250px-Pectoral_of_Senusret_II_by_John_Campana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pectoral_of_Senusret_II_by_John_Campana.jpg)

[Pectoral](/wiki/Pectoral_(Ancient_Egypt) "Pectoral (Ancient Egypt)") of Senusret II (tomb of [Sithathoriunet](/wiki/Sithathoriunet "Sithathoriunet"))

There is an absence of serious evidence for a co-regency between Senusret II and [Senusret III](/wiki/Senusret_III "Senusret III").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansen-Winkeln1997119-43) Murnane identifies that the only existing evidence for a coregency of Senusret II and III is a scarab with both kings names inscribed on it.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurnane19779-44) The association can be explained as being the result of retroactive dating where Senusret II's final regnal year was absorbed into Senusret III's first one, as would be supported by contemporaneous evidence from the Turin Canon which give Senusret II a regnal duration of 19 full regnal years and a partial one.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurnane1977228-45) A dedicatory inscription celebrating the resumption of rituals begun by Senusret II and III, and a papyrus with entries identifying Senusret II's nineteenth regnal year and Senusret III's first regnal year are scant evidence and do not necessitate a coregency.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurnane19779-44) Murnane argues that if there was a coregency, it could not have lasted more than a few months.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurnane19779-44)

The evidence from the papyrus document is now obviated by the fact that the document has been securely dated to Year 19 of [Senusret III](/wiki/Senusret_III "Senusret III") and Year 1 of [Amenemhet III](/wiki/Amenemhet_III "Amenemhet III").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At present, no document from Senusret II's reign has been discovered from Lahun, the king's new capital city.

## Tomb treasure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Tomb treasure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Crown_of_Sit-Hathor_Yunet_%28Senusret_II%27s_daughter%29.jpg/250px-Crown_of_Sit-Hathor_Yunet_%28Senusret_II%27s_daughter%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crown_of_Sit-Hathor_Yunet_(Senusret_II%27s_daughter).jpg)

Crown of Princess [Sithathoriunet](/wiki/Sithathoriunet "Sithathoriunet")

In 1889, the Egyptologist [Flinders Petrie](/wiki/Flinders_Petrie "Flinders Petrie") found "a marvellous gold and inlaid royal [uraeus](/wiki/Uraeus "Uraeus")" that must have originally formed part of Senusret II's looted burial equipment in a flooded chamber of the king's pyramid tomb.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton199480-46) It is now located in the [Egyptian Museum](/wiki/Egyptian_Museum,_Cairo "Egyptian Museum, Cairo"). The tomb of Princess [Sithathoriunet](/wiki/Sithathoriunet "Sithathoriunet"), a daughter of Senusret II, was also discovered in a separate burial site. Several pieces of jewellery from her tomb, including a pair of pectorals and a crown or diadem, were found there. They are now displayed at the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") or the Egyptian Museum.

In 2009, Egyptian archaeologists announced the results of new excavations led by the Egyptologist Abdul Rahman Al-Ayedi. They reported unearthing a cache of pharaonic-era mummies in brightly painted wooden coffins near the Lahun pyramid. The mummies were reportedly the first to be found in the sand-covered desert rock surrounding the pyramid.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

## Pyramid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Senusret_II&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pyramid")]

Main article: [Pyramid of Senusret II](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Senusret_II "Pyramid of Senusret II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/El_Lahun_Pyramid_01.JPG/250px-El_Lahun_Pyramid_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:El_Lahun_Pyramid_01.JPG)

The pyramid of Senusret II at [El-Lahun](/wiki/El-Lahun "El-Lahun")

The pyramid was built around a framework of limestone radial arms, similar to the framework used by Senusret I. Instead of using an infill of stones, mud and mortar, Senusret II used an infill of mud bricks before cladding the structure with a layer of limestone veneer. The outer cladding stones were locked together using dovetail inserts, some of which still remain. A trench was dug around the central core that was filled with stones to act as a [French Drain](/wiki/French_Drain "French Drain"). The limestone cladding stood in this drain, indicating that Senusret II was concerned with water damage.

There were eight mastabas and one small pyramid to the north of Senusret's complex and all were within the enclosure wall. The wall had been encased in limestone that was decorated with niches, perhaps as a copy of Djoser's complex at Saqqara. The mastabas were solid and no chambers have found within or beneath, indicating that they were cenotaphs and possibly symbolic in nature. Flinders Petrie investigated the auxiliary pyramid and found no chambers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Limestone_slab_showing_the_cartouche_of_Senusret_II_and_name_and_image_of_goddess_Nekhbet._From_Mastaba_4%2C_north_side_of_Senusret_II_Pyramid_at_Lahun%2C_Egypt._The_Petrie_Museum_of_Egyptian_Archaeology%2C_London.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limestone_slab_showing_the_cartouche_of_Senusret_II_and_name_and_image_of_goddess_Nekhbet._From_Mastaba_4,_north_side_of_Senusret_II_Pyramid_at_Lahun,_Egypt._The_Petrie_Museum_of_Egyptian_Archaeology,_London.jpg)

Limestone slab showing the cartouche of Senusret II and the name and image of the goddess Nekhbet. From Mastaba 4, north side of Senusret II Pyramid at Lahun, Egypt. [Petrie Museum](/wiki/Petrie_Museum "Petrie Museum"), London.

The entrances to the underground chambers were on the southern side of the pyramid, which confused Flinders Petrie for some months as he looked for the entrance on the traditional northern side.

The builders' vertical access shaft had been filled in after construction and the chamber made to look like a burial chamber. This was no doubt an attempt to convince tomb robbers to look no further.

A secondary access shaft led to a vaulted chamber and a deep well shaft. This may have been an aspect of the cult of Osiris, although it may have been to find the water table. A passage led northwards, past another lateral chamber and turned westwards. This led to an antechamber and vaulted burial chamber, with a sidechamber to the south. The burial chamber was encircled by a unique series of passages that may have reference to the birth of Osiris. A large sarcophagus was found within the burial chamber; it is larger than the doorway and the tunnels, showing that it was put in position when the chamber was being constructed and it was open to the sky.
The limestone outer cladding of the pyramid was removed by Rameses II so he could re-use the stone for his own use. He left inscriptions that he had done so.
